name: Mahesvarananda
dates_notes: Thirteenth-century South-Indian Krama systematiser, working in the Cōla / early Pāṇḍya cultural sphere; dated by his own references to Abhinavagupta and Kṣemarāja and by his standing as the mature systematiser of the South-Indian Mahārtha lineage.
core_thesis: Non-Vedāntin comparator (mature South-Indian Krama systematiser). Maheśvarānanda is the figure who brings the *Krama / Mahārtha* tradition into systematic form in the South in the thirteenth century. His *Mahārtha-Mañjarī* (with the auto-commentary *Parimala*) is the school's mature theological statement: a sustained articulation of the supreme reality as *Cit* in the form of Kālī as *kāla-saṅkarṣiṇī*, whose worship through the *krama* (sequence) of twelve Kālīs is the path of recognition. Maheśvarānanda integrates the South-Indian Krama tradition (built on Abhinanda's source-verses) with the Kashmirian Trika synthesis of Abhinavagupta and Kṣemarāja: the *krama*-doctrine is articulated within the *Pratyabhijñā* analysis of consciousness as *prakāśa-vimarśa*, and the *spanda*-doctrine is integrated as the dynamism of the *krama*-pulse. The signature thesis is that every cognitive moment exhibits the twelve-fold *krama* of Kālīs as the structure of consciousness's self-recognition: manifestation, sustaining, withdrawal, re-emergence, and the recursive levels of recognition that follow are themselves the goddess's own act of self-knowing. The work is the South-Indian Krama's most complete philosophical statement and the principal document by which the Krama tradition survives the disruption of its original Kashmirian context.

engaged_works.title: Mahartha-Manjari with Parimala
engaged_works.ascription_notes: The Parimala auto-commentary is securely authored by Maheśvarānanda; the underlying Mahārtha-Mañjarī verses are Abhinanda's, with Maheśvarānanda's Parimala constructed on them as their authoritative auto-commentary within the same lineage.

ascription_evidence.description: Modern academic consensus (Cox, Sanderson, Silburn studies).
Universal acceptance within the South-Indian Krama lineage as the school's authoritative systematic statement.

engaged_works.summary: Maheśvarānanda's commentary on the seventy-one *gāthās* of Abhinanda's *Mahārtha-Mañjarī*, organised so that the commentary works each verse into a systematic exposition of the *Krama / Mahārtha* tradition. The *Parimala* is the South-Indian Krama's most complete theological work. The *bhūmikā* establishes the foundational metaphysics: *Cit* in the form of Kālī as *kāla-saṅkarṣiṇī*; the integration of the *krama*-doctrine with the Kashmirian *Pratyabhijñā* and *Spanda* programmes (Maheśvarānanda is precise about his debts to Abhinavagupta and Kṣemarāja); the twelve-fold *krama* of Kālīs as the structure exhibited in every cognitive moment. Subsequent verses are commented to develop the school's distinctive doctrines: the Krama analysis of cognition's rising-sustaining-withdrawing-re-emerging rhythm; the soteriological *upāyas* by which the practitioner aligns awareness with the *krama*; the integration of devotional, ritual, and gnostic registers in the worship of Kālī. The work also preserves the school's living memory of the earlier Krama lineage — the Kashmirian Krama figures whom the South-Indian tradition received from but whose own works are largely lost — and is therefore one of the principal sources for the historical reconstruction of the Krama tradition as a whole. The Sanskrit register is mature scholastic, with the Prakrit *mūla* integrated through Maheśvarānanda's running gloss.

reception_notes: Maheśvarānanda is the mature systematiser of the South-Indian Krama tradition; his Mahārtha-Mañjarī with Parimala is the principal document by which the Krama survives into the medieval and early-modern periods after the disruption of the Kashmirian Krama lineage. Modern scholarship (Whitney Cox's monograph, Sanderson's Krama studies, Lilian Silburn's translations) has placed Maheśvarānanda at the centre of the South-Indian Krama lineage and treated the Mahārtha-Mañjarī as the school's standard reference.
